hypotheses: The more a prospect’s frame is associated with risk and the lower the prospect’s lowest possible outcome, the lower people’s willingness to pay (WTP); the prospect’s highest possible outcome will have little effect on WTP.
Willingness to accept (WTA) will not be affected by how the prospect is framed but will depend on a prospect’s lowest and highest possible outcomes.
claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction and Theoretical Background sections (pages 725–727). Specifically: introduction paragraph beginning 'In four experiments, we hypothesize...' and the paragraph beginning 'Our bad-deal aversion account leads to two key predictions:' in Theoretical Background (pages 725–726).
claim.statement: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept

data.source: Participants recruited via Amazon.com's Mechanical Turk (MTurk); experiments using incentivized WTP/WTA elicitations (random-selection incentive-compatible implementation).
data.wave_or_subset: Not applicable / not stated; studies are separate experiments (four between-subjects experiments).
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: N = 119; Experiment 2: N = 418; Experiment 3: N = 533; Experiment 4: N = 605 (as reported in the paper).
data.access_details: Paper reports MTurk as participant source and describes incentive-compatible implementation (random selection of participants to receive payments). No public dataset or code repository link for raw data is provided in the article; an online supplement / DOI for the article is listed (http://dx.doi.org/10.1509/jmr.12.0430).
data.notes: Analyses typically included only participants who passed comprehension checks; payment to participants was $0.20 for taking the HIT in all experiments; some analyses exclude respondents who failed a comprehension question about the lowest possible outcome. WTP/WTA elicitation used a reservation-price implementation (random selection of respondents to actually transact). Pretests for risk association of frames were performed. Various exclusion criteria and instructional manipulation checks were used consistently across experiments.

method.description: Across four between-subjects experiments, participants recruited from MTurk were randomly assigned to conditions that manipulated the verbal frame used to describe prospective payoffs (e.g., 'lottery ticket', 'gift certificate', 'voucher', 'coin flip', 'raffle', 'gamble'), the payoff outcomes (low/high values), endowment status (buyer vs. seller), and objective uncertainty (risky vs. certain). Participants reported their willingness to pay (WTP) if not endowed or willingness to accept (WTA) if endowed. The studies used comprehension checks and an incentive-compatible mechanism in which a subset of participants were randomly selected and faced a reservation price implementation to realize transactions. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, t-tests, nonparametric tests (Mann–Whitney, H tests), and planned contrasts to test main effects and interactions.
steps: Pretest frames for perceived association with risk (seven-point scales) to classify frames as high- or low-risk-associated.
Recruit participants via Amazon MTurk and pay participation fee (reported as $0.20).
Randomly assign participants to experimental conditions varying frame label, payoff outcomes (low/high levels), endowment status (buyer vs. seller), and objective uncertainty where applicable.
Present instructions (as in appendices) including the specific frame and payoff structure; include an instructional manipulation check and comprehension question about lowest possible outcome.

Elicit WTP (for buyers) or WTA (for sellers) using highest willingness to pay / lowest willingness to accept questions; implement incentive-compatible mechanism by randomly selecting a subset of participants and applying a reservation price to determine actual transactions.
Exclude or analyze separately participants failing comprehension checks as reported; report all measures and follow pre-specified exclusion criteria.

Analyze data using ANOVA for factorial designs, t-tests for pairwise comparisons, and nonparametric tests (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis H) where distributions deviated from normality; report effect sizes and significance levels.
method.models: ANOVA (various factorial designs: e.g., 2x3x2, 6x2, 6x2), t-tests for pairwise comparisons, Mann–Whitney U tests and Kruskal–Wallis H tests for nonparametric comparisons, planned contrasts. Reported effect-size measures include partial eta-squared (ηp2).
method.outcome_variable: Willingness to Pay (WTP) and Willingness to Accept (WTA) reported in dollars
method.independent_variables: Frame label (e.g., lottery ticket, gift certificate, voucher, coin flip, raffle, gamble), payoff outcomes (low and high amounts; experimentally varied), endowment status (buyer vs. seller), objective uncertainty (certain low outcome vs. risky prospect).
method.control_variables: Processing disfluency (included as covariate in Experiment 4 but not significant), demographic questions (collected), and exclusion based on instructional manipulation and comprehension checks.
method.tools_software: Not stated (no specific statistical software or code packages are mentioned in the paper).

results.summary: Across four experiments, frames that are more strongly associated with risk (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) substantially reduced WTP compared with frames less associated with risk (gift certificate, voucher). This framing effect influenced WTP but not WTA. Buyers' WTP was sensitive to the prospect's lowest possible outcome but not to its highest outcome; sellers' WTA was sensitive to both low and high outcomes but insensitive to frame. The framing effect on WTP occurred regardless of the objective level of uncertainty and can produce the uncertainty effect (risky prospect valued less than its worst possible outcome) when frames for risky prospects and their low outcomes are mismatched.

numerical_results.effect_size: partial eta-squared = .11 (reported for t-test comparison context)
numerical_results.direction: WTP lower under lottery frame (negative effect of risk-associated frame on WTP)

numerical_results.value: Frame main effect F(1,383) = 6.88, p = .009; Outcome main effect F(2,383) = 34.52, p < .001; Status main effect F(1,383) = 76.87, p < .001; Status x Frame interaction F(1,383) = 6.33, p = .012
numerical_results.effect_size: partial eta-squared reported for main effects (e.g., ηp2 = .02 for frame; .15 for outcome; .17 for status)
numerical_results.direction: Frame reduces WTP (buyers) but not WTA (sellers); outcomes affect WTP and WTA differently

numerical_results.outcome_name: Experiment 2: WTA sensitivity to high outcome
numerical_results.value: WTA when high outcome $200: M = $74.45 vs when $100: M = $50.21
numerical_results.direction: WTA higher with higher high-outcome (positive effect of high outcome on WTA)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Experiment 2: WTP insensitive to high outcome (when low fixed at $50)
Experiment 2: Sensitivity to low outcome (WTP and WTA)
numerical_results.effect_size: WTP ηp2 = .17 for t-test; WTA ηp2 = .10 for t-test
numerical_results.p_value: p < .001 for both WTP and WTA comparisons
numerical_results.direction: Lower low outcome reduces both WTP and WTA (negative effect)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Experiment 3: Frame x Status interaction and WTP differences
numerical_results.value: Frame main effect F(5,459) = 9.34, p < .001; Frame x Status interaction F(5,459) = 2.64, p = .02; WTP differed across frames F(5,225) = 11.63, p < .001; WTA did not differ significantly across frames F(5,234) = 1.95, p = .09
numerical_results.effect_size: ηp2 for frame = .09; for WTP across frames ηp2 = .21 reported
numerical_results.direction: Frames associated with risk lowered WTP; WTA unaffected by frame

numerical_results.outcome_name: Experiment 4: Objective uncertainty and framing effects on WTP
numerical_results.value: Objective uncertainty main effect F(1,428) = 5.09, p = .025 (risky prospect M = $33.64, SD = $21.33; certain low outcome M = $29.60, SD = $17.16); Frame main effect F(5,428) = 14.73, p < .001; interaction not significant
numerical_results.effect_size: ηp2 reported for frame effects and contrasts (e.g., ηp2 = .10 and .18 for planned contrasts)
numerical_results.direction: Frames associated with risk reduced WTP regardless of objective uncertainty; framing can produce the uncertainty effect when frames mismatch

metadata.original_paper_title: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept
metadata.original_paper_data: Online supplement / article DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.1509/jmr.12.0430 (no direct dataset link provided in the paper)
